name: Snipcart
what_it_does: JavaScript shopping cart SaaS with a REST API for managing products, orders, customers, subscriptions, and discounts on any website.
use_cases: Add a fully functional shopping cart and checkout flow to a static site or JAMstack app with a few lines of HTML
Query and manage orders programmatically via REST API for fulfillment automation
Retrieve customer purchase history and lifetime value for CRM or marketing integrations
Create and manage discount codes and subscription products via API without touching the storefront
not_for: High-volume merchants needing deep inventory management, PIM, or warehouse logistics integrations
Teams that need a standalone payment processor; Snipcart requires Stripe, PayPal, or another gateway underneath
best_when: Best when developers need to add e-commerce to an existing website without migrating to a full platform like Shopify or WooCommerce.
avoid_when: Avoid when transaction volume makes the 2% fee model more expensive than a flat-rate SaaS platform.

auth.notes: Management API uses HTTP Basic Auth with the secret API key as the username and an empty password. Public API key is embedded in the frontend JS snippet for cart initialization. No scope granularity.

pricing.free_tier_limits: No free tier; test mode available for development
pricing.paid_tiers: 2% transaction fee on all sales with a minimum of $10/month; no per-seat cost
pricing.notes: Transaction-fee model is simple but can become expensive at scale. Minimum $10/month applies even on low-volume months.

performance.rate_limits: API rate limits not publicly documented; standard SaaS throttling applies

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API returns standard HTTP status codes with JSON error descriptions. Error messages are generally clear for common failure modes.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Order reads are safe and idempotent. Write operations such as refunds are not idempotent and can be duplicated if retried without checking state.
known_agent_gotchas: Domain verification is required; the API key is locked to specific domains making sandbox testing require domain configuration
Secret API key and public API key serve different purposes; using the wrong one results in silent failures in the cart
No SDK means agents must implement HTTP Basic Auth and JSON parsing manually

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTP Basic Auth with API key is simple but lacks scope control. PCI-DSS compliance for payment handling. Domain locking adds some security. Secret key exposure would grant full account access.
